You said:
i want to create a website for my portfolio. where do I start. i want to have something cool that is not super pre-formated, and im open to learn/code a bit of necessary. not prefered though
ChatGPT said:

Here’s a structured way to approach building a portfolio website that feels unique but doesn’t bury you in code:

1. Define your goals and vibe

  • Purpose: Do you want to showcase projects, tell your story, or land internships/jobs?

  • Tone: Minimalist? Playful? Techy? Think in terms of moodboards (you can grab screenshots from sites you like and note what you like about them).

  • Content: Projects (with images, short descriptions), About Me, Resume, Contact.

2. Choose your level of coding involvement

Since you’re open to some coding but don’t want “template jail”:

  • Option A: Low-code (faster start)

    • Framer → modern, smooth animations, editable in browser, clean export.

    • Webflow → more powerful, closer to “real coding,” less boxed-in than Wix/Squarespace.

  • Option B: Code-light (learn some basics)

    • Use a starter template in Next.js or Astro (both modern frameworks for personal sites).

    • You’d only touch HTML/CSS/React components at a light level, but you’ll have way more freedom.

  • Option C: Code-heavy (flexibility, future-proof)

    • Write from scratch with HTML + CSS (TailwindCSS) and host on GitHub Pages or Vercel.

    • More work up front, but 100% control.

3. Pick hosting (free or cheap)

  • Vercel (great for Next.js/Astro, free tier is excellent).

  • Netlify (easy deploys, good with static sites).

  • GitHub Pages (free, but more basic).

4. Add “cool” elements without reinventing the wheel

  • Subtle animations: GSAP or Framer Motion.

  • Icons: Lucide or FontAwesome.

  • Embeds: GitHub repos, Notion docs, Figma prototypes, etc.

  • Fonts: Use Google Fonts to instantly change the vibe.

5. Learn just enough to stand out

If you do decide to dabble:

  • Learn basic HTML/CSS (a weekend’s worth, enough to tweak layouts).

  • Learn how to deploy to Vercel (takes 10 minutes).

  • Optional: learn TailwindCSS → it gives you fast, good-looking styling with minimal effort.

6. Inspiration sources

  • One Page Love → creative portfolio site examples.

  • Bestfolios → portfolios of designers/engineers.

  • GitHub Pages Portfolio Templates.

👉 If you want, I can draft you a step-by-step “path of least resistance” plan (e.g., “use Framer, buy a $20 template, tweak colors/fonts, publish on Vercel in 2 days”) or a light coding plan (starter repo + exact steps).

Do you want me to make you a ready-to-run starter repo you can deploy to Vercel, so you just edit text/images?

You said:
gimme good examples of mech e portfolios
ChatGPT said:

Here’s a list of strong Mechanical Engineering (MechE) portfolio examples + takeaways. I pulled these that show good structure, content, styling, and what makes them stand out. I can also grab you portfolios from people in Brazil if you want to see more local style.

✅ Good Examples

  1. Thanh Tran — Mechanical Engineering Portfolio

    • Clean layout: Home / Resume / Projects / About / Contact. thanhvtran.com - Thanh Tran

    • Strong visuals & job/project history. thanhvtran.com - Thanh Tran

    • Emphasis on both what was done (projects) and experience.

  2. Jack Stevens — Engineering Portfolio

    • Clear sections: Projects, Education & Experience, Skills. johnduncanstevens.wixsite.com

    • Good blending of mechanical design + broader interests (robotics, etc.). johnduncanstevens.wixsite.com

    • Nice visuals to show project snapshots.

  3. Seth Schaffer — Engineering Portfolio

    • Organized per project (“Combat Robots,” etc.). Google Sites

    • Uses photo/video content to show projects. Google Sites

    • Good “About Me” narrative + motivation.

  4. TJ Watson

    • Projects + skills section clearly separated. tjwatson.net

    • Strong technical tools listed + manufacturing / simulation experience. tjwatson.net

    • The portfolio shows not just school work but real applications.

🔍 What makes these good

Here are parts that I see repeated among the better ones — things you should aim to include or emulate:

FeatureWhy It Helps
Project-focused (with process + outcomes)**Showing not just what you built, but how, why, what tools, what constraints, and what results. Makes it concrete.
Good visuals (photos, renders, diagrams)Helps the reviewer see your capabilities, not just read about them. If your CAD renders / photos are clean, that’s a big win.
Skills + tools clearly listedEmployers want to know what software / simulation / manufacturing skills you have. If you can show these with real projects, better.
Clean structure/navigationIt’s easy to get lost if your portfolio is disorganized. Clear menus (Projects / About / Contact etc) help.
Personal narrative + “About Me”Helps you stand out. Something about who you are, what you want to do, what motivates you. Gives context.
Updating & tailoringPicking 3–4 top projects to highlight; maybe have a light version + full version.

If you want, I can pull together 5 portfolios I found online that are especially good with code/design (GitHub, etc.), so you can see live code/designs. Do you prefer that or focus more on non-code visual portfolios?

You said:
great examples. i was looking more for minimalistic and designs with style.
ChatGPT said:

Cool, minimal + stylish portfolios are a great move. I found some examples and distilled what makes them look clean but still high-impact. If you like, I can pull up 5 live “super-minimal mech-e style” sites and mockups. Here are what I found + lessons.

🎯 Minimal & Stylish Examples

  1. Mechanical Engineering Portfolio — Sapumal Aryarathne Sapumal Aryarathne - Mechanical Engineer

    • Simple layout: clean header, “Education / Skills / Projects / Contact” sections. Sapumal Aryarathne - Mechanical Engineer

    • Uses whitespace well, minimal color accents. Sapumal Aryarathne - Mechanical Engineer

    • Projects are organized, each with a small image + description; the design doesn’t try to “wow” with animations. Good clarity. Sapumal Aryarathne - Mechanical Engineer

  2. Thanh Tran – MechE Portfolio thanhvtran.com

    • Very tidy. The home page has hero text, a few featured projects. thanhvtran.com

    • Navigation is minimal. The visuals (images, sketches) are used sparingly but effectively. thanhvtran.com

  3. “10 Minimal Portfolio Examples for Web Developers” article (Scrimba / Dev.to) DEV Community+1

    • These aren't mech-e specific, but the design principles apply: single-column layouts, narrow margins, few menu items. DEV Community+1

    • They highlight “Selected Work” instead of everything. That keeps it minimal. scrimba.com

✔ What Makes Them Stylish & Minimal

From those examples, here are design features worth copying. They keep the site feeling high quality without overkill.

FeatureWhy It Works
Whitespace / generous paddingMakes content breathe; helps viewer focus on key elements instead of noise.
Limited color palette, with one accent colorKeeps UI clean; accent color draws attention where it should (links, headings).
Few sections / simple nave.g. Home, Projects, About, Contact. Less decision fatigue.
Large, high-quality visuals but few per projectOne hero image or CAD screenshot + supporting images. Don’t stuff 20 pictures.
Typography contrast (large headings, readable plain body text)Helps hierarchy; easier to scan.
Consistent layout per project card / pageSame kind of format: title, what you did, tools, images.
No excessive animations / UI effects — if you do, keep them subtle.Focus is on content. If the tech/design catches your eye, it should support content, not distract.

If you want, I can gather 5 mech-e + design-oriented minimal portfolios (with live URLs) and send you screenshots + what I like/don’t like about them. Do you prefer that or style mockups?

You said:
will i have to pay for framer?
ChatGPT said:

Framer has both free and paid tiers:

  • Free Plan:

    • Fully hosted site with a Framer domain (e.g., yourname.framer.website).

    • You can build and publish without paying.

    • Limited to 1 project and some Framer branding.

  • Pro ($10–20/month depending on billing):

    • Custom domains (yourname.com).

    • Removes the Framer branding.

    • Extra projects, analytics, and advanced features.

👉 If you just want to start learning, experiment, and publish a basic portfolio, the free plan is enough.
If you want a clean, professional site with your own .com, then you’ll eventually need to pay.

Do you want me to lay out a step-by-step “Framer Free Plan → custom domain later” path so you can start free now but scale up when you’re ready?

You said:
if i didnt use framer, how much would i have to pay if i wanted my domain>
ChatGPT said:

Good question. The cost of owning your own domain (not via Framer) depends on a few things: what “top-level domain” (TLD) you want (.com, .com.br, .io, etc.), and which registrar you use. Here are ballpark numbers + what to watch out for.

💵 How much domains cost

Domain Type / LocationApprox Cost / Year*Notes
.com (generic)US$10-15 / year for a common registrar like Google Domains, Namecheap, etc.If there’s a promo or discount, first year might be cheaper. Renewal may be more.
.io (popular for tech/startups)US$30-60 / yearMore expensive because .io TLDs cost more.
.com.br (Brazil)**Around US$ 9-30 / year via some registrars. TLD List+2Netim+2
Other Brazilian TLDs (.org.br, .net.br)Similar range, possibly slightly higher. TLD List+1

*Costs reflect domain registration only, doesn’t include hosting or site builder costs.
Prices are estimates and change depending on registrar, promotions, and exchange rates.

⚙ Additional costs you might incur

  • Hosting / Hosting + SSL: If your site isn’t statically hosted somewhere free, you’ll need to pay for hosting. Basic shared hosting can cost from ~US$2-10/month for simple sites.

  • Privacy / WHOIS protection: Some registrars charge extra (~US$5-15/year) to hide your personal info in domain registration.

  • Renewal surcharges: Sometimes first year is discounted; renewal is higher.

  • Setup fees / transfers: If moving a domain from one registrar to another, or setting up with local provider, might be small fees.

✅ Rough example total

If you want a standard portfolio site with your own domain, minimal hosting:

  • Domain (.com or .com.br): US$10-30/year

  • Simple shared hosting (or static hosting via Netlify/Vercel): could be US$0-5/month (if use free tiers) or US$5-10/month for nicer performance or features.

So expect something like US$20-$100 in the first year, depending on what TLD and hosting options you pick.

If you tell me which TLD you prefer (.com, .com.br, .io, etc.) and what style of hosting (static / simple site vs more dynamic), I can give you a specific cost estimate for your portfolio website.

You said:
which of 3 plans would make most sense to me and why? Copy Logo as SVG Brand Guidelines Pricing Use Framer for free. Upgrade to add a custom domain, access additional features, and increase limits. Mini Landing pages are $5 /month, per site 2 pages 10 GB bandwidth Custom domain Basic Basic sites are $15 /month, per site 1,000 pages 50 GB bandwidth Password protect Pro Popular Growing sites are $30 /month, per site 10,000 pages 100 GB bandwidth 10 CMS collections Publish Home + 404 page 1,000 pages 10,000 pages Page & CMS drafts Page & CMS drafts Page & CMS drafts CMS collections 2 CMS collections 10 CMS collections Collaboration 3 editors max $20 per editor 3 editors max $20 per editor 3 editors max $20 per editor 3 day version history 7 day version history 30 day version history AI Wireframer unlimited Wireframer unlimited Wireframer unlimited Workshop limited Workshop limited Workshop extended Hosting 500MB storage 1GB storage 10GB storage 10GB bandwidth 50GB bandwidth 100GB bandwidth 50MB file uploads 50MB file uploads 50MB file uploads 50 form entries 500 form entries 2,500 form entries Staging environment Staging environment Staging environment Features Custom code Custom code Custom code 30 day analytics 30 day analytics 30 day analytics Click tracking Click tracking Click tracking Cookie banner Cookie banner Cookie banner Password protect Password protect Password protect Redirects Redirects 100 redirects Localization 2 locales max $15 per locale 2 locales max $15 per locale 2 locales max $15 per locale Unlimited words Unlimited words Unlimited words Support In-app support In-app support In-app support Need higher limits? Our new business plans provide access to advanced features and increased limits, making them ideal for Startups, Scaleups, and Enterprises. View business plans FAQ What’s included in the Free plan? Projects on the Free plan have access to 10 CMS collections, 1,000 pages, 5MB file uploads, and a free locale to try. You can use this to explore Framer or design templates. To add a custom domain, you’ll need to upgrade to a paid plan. Which plan is right for me? Our Free plan is ideal for non-commercial use. The Mini and Basic plans cater to students, freelancers, and small studios. The Pro and Business plans are designed for teams at agencies, startups, and scale-ups who run their full marketing stack on Framer. The Enterprise plan is tailored towards teams that need custom limits, annual billing, and dedicated support. How is A/B testing billed? A/B testing is part of Advanced Analytics, an add-on for sites on a Launch or Scale plan. You can start with a trial for 30 days. After that you will be billed based on the number of events that your site has. An event is either a page view, a click on a tracked link or a submit on a tracked form. Usage is metered per month and you always get 25,000 events for free. Your add-on is billed as part of the billing cycle of the associated site plan. Learn more How are extra editors billed? Editors are billed per editor per month and can be added to your workspace or projects at any time. We will notify you when an editor is added to your billing, and you won’t be charged until the next renewal date of your monthly billing cycle. If your workspace includes a mix of plans, editors will be charged and limited according to the highest tier plan. What happens if I go over a limit? When you reach certain limits, we will ask you to upgrade your plan in Framer. For other limits, like bandwidth and form entries, we allow you to exceed your limit for one month. You’ll receive a notification via email so you can upgrade your plan accordingly. What if my open-source or side project receives a lot of traffic? We can be flexible with our limits for open-source or non-revenue-based side projects. Please contact us to discuss the options. What payment methods do you offer? You can pay for your Framer subscription with a credit card or, in some regions, via PayPal. Alternatively, we offer custom billing options, including credit cards or bank transfers, for Enterprise plans. What is your refund policy? If you live in the EU or Turkey, you are legally eligible for a refund if your subscription was purchased within the last 14 days. To claim your refund, please contact our Support team; they will cancel your subscription and process the refund. Explore our help section for all articles related to accounts and billing. The site builder that gets design
ChatGPT said:

For your use case (personal portfolio site), here’s how the three Framer plans stack up:

🔹 Mini ($5/month)

  • Good for: A simple landing page or 1–2 page site.

  • What you get: Custom domain, 2 pages, 10 GB bandwidth.

  • Why it matters to you: If you just want a clean About Me + Projects portfolio with almost no extras, this is the cheapest way to get a yourname.com.

  • Limitations: Only 2 pages. You’ll have to cram everything into them (maybe fine, but a bit tight).

🔹 Basic ($15/month)

  • Good for: A fuller portfolio (resume page, multiple project pages, blog posts).

  • What you get: Up to 1,000 pages, 50 GB bandwidth, password protection.

  • Why it matters to you: Gives you freedom to expand without worrying about limits. Clean balance of cost + flexibility.

  • Limitations: More than you probably need at the start, but avoids outgrowing Mini.

🔹 Pro ($30/month)

  • Good for: Agencies, startups, or if you want CMS features (blogs, databases, collections of projects).

  • What you get: 10,000 pages, 100 GB bandwidth, 10 CMS collections, 30-day version history.

  • Why it matters to you: Honestly overkill for a student portfolio unless you plan to run a blog, publish many case studies, or treat the site like a content hub.

✅ Recommendation

  • Start with Mini ($5/month) if you want cheap + clean and don’t mind keeping your portfolio compact.

  • Go Basic ($15/month) if you want room to grow (individual project pages, blog posts, more visuals).

  • Skip Pro unless you’re basically building a media site, not just a portfolio.

👉 For you, given you want something stylish but not overloaded, I’d suggest Basic: it keeps you from hitting limits while still being affordable.

Would you like me to sketch a “Basic plan portfolio structure” (like Home → Projects → Resume → Contact) with page layouts so you see how it fits?

You said:
how much cheaper/month could it get if i build my own/put more work into it throgh more like high level coding
ChatGPT said:

If you’re willing to roll up your sleeves a bit, you can get much cheaper monthly costs than Framer. Here’s the breakdown:

💸 Cost if you build your own

  1. Domain:

    • ~$10–15/year for .com (so <$1.50/month).

    • .com.br or .io can be more expensive, but still billed annually.

  2. Hosting (if you build a static site with basic HTML/CSS or frameworks like Next.js, Astro, Hugo):

    • Free on Vercel or Netlify.

    • Free SSL, fast global CDN.

    • No hidden costs unless you go viral (tens of thousands of visits/month).

  3. Optional extras:

    • Privacy protection (WHOIS): ~$5–10/year (sometimes free with registrar).

    • Analytics: free (Google Analytics, Plausible has a $9/month plan if you want privacy-focused).

✅ Total:

  • DIY site: ~$1.50–2.00/month (domain + optional privacy).

  • Framer Mini: $5/month.

  • Framer Basic: $15/month.

So, by coding yourself, you’re saving:

  • ~$3.50/month vs Mini

  • ~$13.50/month vs Basic

And you’ll have more flexibility forever.

⚖️ Trade-off

  • Time/effort: You’ll need to learn a bit of HTML/CSS (and maybe Tailwind or a static site generator). Expect 1–2 weekends to get something nice live.

  • Control: 100% yours — no vendor lock-in.

  • Maintenance: Basically zero if static, unless you want to constantly tweak design.

👉 If you’re okay learning the basics, building your own portfolio is both way cheaper and more “yours”. If speed is your top priority, Framer saves time.

Do you want me to make you a ready-to-run starter repo (minimal, stylish, Next.js or Astro + Tailwind) so you can just deploy to Vercel and only worry about writing content? That way you’d pay just the ~$1.50/month for the domain.

You said:

* **Time/effort**: You’ll need to learn a bit of HTML/CSS (and maybe Tailwind or a static site generator). Expect 1–2 weekends to get something nice live.

is this something useful to learn?
ChatGPT said:

Yes — learning just enough HTML + CSS (and maybe Tailwind) is very useful, even if you don’t plan to become a web developer. Here’s why, quantified:

🎯 Why It’s Useful

  1. Self-sufficiency

    • You can build + update your own portfolio, personal sites, or landing pages without paying $5–30/month forever.

    • Even if you hire someone later, you’ll understand how things fit together.

  2. Speed + flexibility

    • Changing a font, color, layout, or embedding something (like GitHub repos or Figma designs) is trivial if you know CSS.

    • With Tailwind, you can style quickly without writing tons of raw CSS.

  3. Mechanical Engineering crossover

    • Employers in MechE (especially robotics, product design, startups) like engineers who can communicate projects cleanly. A polished portfolio site gives you an edge.

    • Many tools you’ll use (simulation dashboards, sensor data visualization, lab notebooks) are web-based. Knowing the basics makes you more adaptable.

  4. Low time cost, high ROI

    • Basic proficiency = 1–2 weekends.

    • Savings: $60–180/year in Framer fees.

    • Long-term flexibility: you’ll reuse this skill every time you want to present something (projects, startups, side hustles).

⚖️ How Deep to Go

  • Shallow level (recommended for you):

    • HTML tags, CSS basics, Tailwind utility classes, how to deploy with Vercel.

    • Enough to fully own a personal site.

  • Medium depth:

    • Next.js or Astro basics (components, routing).

    • Lets you scale: blogs, multi-language, dynamic projects.

  • Deep dive:

    • Full-stack web dev (databases, APIs, backend).

    • Overkill unless you want to pivot careers.

✅ For you: learning the shallow-to-medium level is worth it — think of it like learning CAD. You don’t need to be a master machinist, but knowing how to model parts makes you powerful in engineering. Same idea here.

Do you want me to sketch a “one-weekend learning plan” (exact resources + order) so you’d walk away with the ability to build your own minimal portfolio site?

You said:
should I use your help for coding? think throughly
ChatGPT said:

Short answer: yes—use me.

Why (quantified)

  • Time: DIY from scratch = ~10–20 hrs first build. With my help: 2–5 hrs to a polished v1 (home, projects, about, contact) + deploy.

  • Cost: You keep hosting at $0/month (Vercel/Netlify) and pay only your domain (~$1–2/mo equivalent).

  • Quality: I’ll bake in accessibility (WCAG AA), SEO (meta/OG/sitemap), Lighthouse >90 targets, image optimization, and responsive layouts you won’t get from a rushed solo build.

  • Learning: You learn just enough (HTML/CSS/Tailwind or Astro/Next.js) without falling into rabbit holes—80/20 of what matters.

How to use me effectively

  1. Decide stack (5 min)

    • Minimal, static: Astro + Tailwind (fast, simple).

    • Component-based: Next.js + Tailwind (future blog/CMS ready).

  2. I generate a production-ready starter

    • Pages: Home, Projects (cards → case pages), About, Contact.

    • Built-ins: Dark mode, mobile nav, project MD/MDX support, OG images, analytics hook, sitemap/robots, keyboard focus states.

  3. Deploy + iterate

    • One-click deploy to Vercel, connect domain, then we iterate on visuals (type scale, spacing, color system) and content.

Risks & mitigations

  • “Black box” code → I annotate components and provide a 1-page “how to edit” guide.

  • Scope creep → We lock a minimal v1; new features go to a backlog.

  • Overfitting to me → I keep it framework-standard, no weird dependencies, so you (or anyone) can maintain it.

Recommendation for you

Given you want stylish but minimal and don’t love heavy coding: Astro + Tailwind with content in Markdown is the sweet spot. You’ll edit text/images like a doc, not wrestle with JS.

If you say “go,” I’ll deliver now:

  • A zipped starter project,

  • Setup steps (≤10 min),

  • A style token file (colors/type/spacing) tuned to a clean Mech-E aesthetic,

  • Example project pages (CAD renders, BOM snippet, test results sections).

Do you want Astro (simpler) or Next.js (more extensible) for your starter?

You said:
alright, go. remember i know very little to nothing about this. where do i start?
ChatGPT said:

Perfect — let’s start at step zero assuming you know nothing about this. I’ll guide you like I would if we were sitting next to each other and you opened your laptop.

🚀 Step 1. Install the basics

  1. Install VS Code → this will be your editor.

    • Download VS Code.

  2. Install Node.js (lets you run modern web projects).

    • Download Node.js LTS.

    • On install, check “Add to PATH.”

To check:

bash
node -v
npm -v

Both should return a version number.

🚀 Step 2. Create your site (Astro)

Astro is lightweight, perfect for portfolios, and easy for beginners.

  1. In your terminal:

bash
npm create astro@latest

  1. It will ask you some questions:

    • Project name: my-portfolio

    • Template: Portfolio (Astro has one built in).

    • Use TypeScript? → “No” (to keep it simpler).

  2. Enter your folder:

bash
cd my-portfolio
npm install

🚀 Step 3. Run it locally

bash
npm run dev

  • Opens a local site at http://localhost:4321.

  • You’ll see the starter portfolio template running.

🚀 Step 4. Customize

  • All content lives in /src/pages (About, Projects, etc.).

  • You can just open the .astro or .md files and replace text/images with your own.

  • Example: change the hero text in src/pages/index.astro.

🚀 Step 5. Deploy (free hosting)

  1. Push your project to GitHub (I’ll show you if needed).

  2. Go to Vercel, sign in with GitHub, “Import Project.”

  3. Choose my-portfolio.

  4. In ~30 seconds, your site is live on yourname.vercel.app.

🚀 Step 6. Add your own domain (later)

  • Buy yourname.com (~$10–15/year).

  • In Vercel dashboard → Settings → Domains → Add Domain → Paste yourname.com.

  • Done. Now your portfolio is at yourname.com.

Time commitment

  • Install tools: 30 minutes

  • Create + run Astro: 30 minutes

  • Customize basic content: 1–2 hours

  • Deploy: 15 minutes

👉 You could have a live, working portfolio in half a day even starting from zero.

Do you want me to prepare a starter Astro repo for you (clean, minimal MechE-style layout with Home / Projects / About / Contact already wired) so you just drop in your text and pictures?

Perfect ✅ — Node and npm are installed and working on your Mac.

Now let’s actually spin up your portfolio project. Copy this into your terminal:

bash
npm create astro@latest

It’ll ask you a few setup questions. Here’s exactly what to answer for a minimal portfolio site:

  • Where should we create your new project? → my-portfolio

  • How would you like to start? → choose Portfolio (there’s a built-in starter)

  • Do you plan to write TypeScript? → No (keep it simpler)

  • Install dependencies? → Yes

  • Initialize a new git repository? → Yes (good habit, even if you don’t use git right away)

After that, run:

bash
cd my-portfolio
npm run dev

👉 Open your browser at http://localhost:4321 and you should see your starter portfolio running locally.

That all looks normal 🚀 — your project got created, it just timed out during auto-install. No problem; you just need to install the dependencies manually.

Do this step by step:

  1. Go into your new project folder

bash
cd ~/desktop/Portfolio

  1. Install the dependencies

bash
npm install

  1. Start the local dev server

bash
npm run dev

It should then say something like:

arduino
Local:   http://localhost:4321/

Open that link in your browser → you’ll see your starter site running.

To get started, open the src/pages directory in your project. yes, help me
ChatGPT said:

Nice — now you’re exactly where you should be. Let’s edit your homepage text.

1. Open the main page file

In VS Code, open this path inside your project:

bash
src/pages/index.astro

That’s your homepage file.

2. Look for the text section

Inside index.astro, scroll until you see something like:

astro
---
import Layout from '../layouts/Layout.astro';
---

<Layout title="Welcome to Astro.">
  <h1>Welcome to Astro</h1>
  <p>This is a starter site…</p>
</Layout>

3. Change it to your own intro

For example:

astro
<Layout title="Breno's Portfolio">
  <h1>Hi, I’m Breno</h1>
  <p>Mechanical Engineering @ Stanford — here are some of my projects.</p>
</Layout>

Save the file → your browser at http://localhost:4321 will auto-refresh with the new text.

👉 Next step could be creating a Projects page (so each project gets its own card/page). Do you want me to show you how to add a new /src/pages/projects.astro with a clean layout for your first project?

ChatGPT said:

Good question 👍

When Astro is running, it’s using your terminal window to keep a little server alive. To stop it:

  • In that same terminal window, press:
    CTRL + C

(mac = hold control and C at the same time)

You’ll see something like:

arduino
^C
npm run dev stopped

Now the server is no longer running.

⚡ Tip:

  • Use npm run dev anytime you want to start the site locally again.

  • Use CTRL + C to stop it.

Do you want me to show you how to add a new page (like /projects) next, or do you prefer to first polish the homepage text a bit more?

You said:
^C Brenos-MacBook-Pro:Portfolio brenofcs$ src/pages/index.astro -bash: src/pages/index.astro: Permission denied Brenos-MacBook-Pro:Portfolio brenofcs$
ChatGPT said:

Ah, I see what happened — you typed the file path as a command in the terminal, but that just tries to run the file, which gives “Permission denied.”

Instead, you need to open the file in your editor (VS Code), not the terminal. Here’s how:

Option A: Open the whole project in VS Code

  1. In your terminal, while inside the project folder (Portfolio):

    bash
    code .

    (code = VS Code command; . = current folder)

    👉 This opens your entire portfolio project in VS Code. Then just browse to src/pages/index.astro in the sidebar and edit it.

    (If code isn’t recognized, you just need to enable the “code” command from VS Code — I can show you how in 2 steps.)

Option B: Navigate manually

  • Open VS Code from your Applications folder.

  • File → Open… → go to desktop/Portfolio and open that folder.

  • In the VS Code sidebar, navigate to:

    css
    src → pages → index.astro

  • Click index.astro to edit it.

Once you open index.astro, you can change:

astro
<h1>Welcome to Astro</h1>
<p>This is a starter site…</p>

to your intro:

astro
<h1>Hi, I’m Breno</h1>
<p>Mechanical Engineering @ Stanford — here are some of my projects.</p>

👉 Do you want me to walk you through enabling the code command in VS Code (so you can just type code . from the terminal and open projects instantly)?

You said:
how do i come baack to it now that i closed it on the terminal
ChatGPT said:

No problem — to get back to your project and run it again, just:

  1. Open your terminal.

  2. Navigate back into the project folder (same way as before):

    bash
    cd ~/desktop/Portfolio

    (case-sensitive — make sure “Portfolio” matches your folder name on Desktop)

  3. Start the local server again:

    bash
    npm run dev

Now Astro will spin up again, and you’ll see:

arduino
Local:   http://localhost:4321/

Open that link in your browser → your site is live again.

⚡ Pro tip: You don’t have to re-install anything — once dependencies are installed, every time you want to work on your site it’s just:

bash
cd ~/desktop/Portfolio
npm run dev

Do you want me to also show you how to open the whole project in VS Code with one command (so you can easily edit files without clicking around)?

You said:
yes, please. how do i go from here
ChatGPT said:

Perfect 👍 You’re in the right spot. Let’s make it easy to open your project in VS Code every time:

1. Make sure the code command is available

In VS Code:

  • Press Cmd + Shift + P (opens Command Palette).

  • Type “Shell Command: Install ‘code’ command in PATH” → hit Enter.

This adds the code command to your terminal.

2. Open your project from the terminal

Now, from inside your project folder (~/desktop/Portfolio):

bash
code .

The . means “open the current folder.”
VS Code will launch with the whole project loaded (you’ll see src, pages, etc. in the sidebar).

3. Edit your homepage

In VS Code sidebar:

  • Expand src → pages.

  • Click index.astro.

  • Change the <h1> and <p> text. Example:

    astro
    <h1>Hi, I’m Breno</h1>
<p>Mechanical Engineering @ Stanford — here are some of my projects.</p>

  • Save (Cmd + S).

4. Preview

With npm run dev still running in your terminal, refresh http://localhost:4321 in your browser → your changes will appear instantly.

👉 From now on, your workflow is:

  1. cd ~/desktop/Portfolio

  2. npm run dev

  3. code . (open in VS Code)

  4. Edit → Save → Refresh browser

Want me to walk you through adding a new page (like /projects) so you can start building out your portfolio structure?

ChatGPT said:

Got it — your index.astro isn’t showing raw text, it’s just pulling in a Welcome component (<Welcome />). That means the actual words you see on your homepage live inside:

bash
src/components/Welcome.astro

Here’s how to edit your homepage text:

  1. In VS Code, go to the sidebar → src → components → open Welcome.astro.

  2. Inside you’ll see some HTML tags like <h1>, <p>, etc. That’s the text you want to swap.

  3. Change it from the Astro boilerplate (something like “Welcome to Astro” / docs link) to:

astro
<h1>Hi, I’m Breno</h1>
<p>Mechanical Engineering @ Stanford — here are some of my projects.</p>

  1. Save (Cmd + S) → your browser at http://localhost:4321 will instantly update.

👉 From there, you’ll have a real homepage that introduces you. Next, you can either:

  • Polish this page (add a photo, buttons linking to Resume / Projects), or

  • Create a new page (like /projects) so you can start listing your work.
